signals.headline: Ex-Syrian intelligence chief found guilty of torture and sexual abuse by Austrian court
signals.summary: A former Syrian intelligence chief in the ​city of Raqqa has been found guilty of torture and sexual abuse of opponents of the former Syrian leader Bashar al-Assad, at a court in Vienna, Austria.
keyTakeaways: A second Syrian official, the former police chief in Raqqa, was also found guilty of abusing political opponents.
The former intelligence chief, named only as Khaled al-H.
Khaled al-H., a member of the Druze ethno-religious minority, was found guilty of committing torture.
signals.whyItMatters: The Austrian Press Agency says the agreement was overseen by an Austrian official, Martin Weiss, the former head of the (BVT).

ja.headline: 元シリア情報機関幹部に有罪判決――ウィーンの法廷で
ja.summary: オーストリアの首都ウィーンの裁判所が、シリアのラッカ市で情報機関を率いていた元幹部に有罪判決を下した。アサド前政権への反対者に対する拷問と性的虐待が認定された。
keyTakeaways: ラッカの元警察署長も同様に、政治的反対者への虐待で有罪とされた。
有罪となった元情報機関幹部は、「ハーレド・アル＝H」とのみ公表されている。
ドゥルーズ派の宗教的少数民族に属する同人物には、拷問の罪が認定された。
ja.whyItMatters: オーストリア通信によれば、この合意はオーストリア当局者のマルティン・ヴァイス元BVT長官の監督のもとで締結されたという。

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw something about a Syrian official being convicted in Austria. What happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: A former Syrian intelligence chief from the city of Raqqa was found guilty by a court in Vienna. He was convicted of torture and sexual abuse of people who opposed Bashar al-Assad.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Wait, how did an Austrian court end up trying someone for crimes in Syria?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: That's a good question. Some countries have laws allowing them to prosecute serious human rights crimes regardless of where they happened. Austria appears to be using that authority here.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Was he the only one on trial?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: No. A second Syrian official was also found guilty—the former police chief in Raqqa. He was convicted of abusing political opponents as well.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Do we know anything about the intelligence chief?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: He's identified only as Khaled al-H. He's a member of the Druze ethno-religious minority and was convicted specifically of committing torture.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: This must be part of a broader effort to hold people accountable for what happened in Syria.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It does seem that way. Courts in Europe have been increasingly willing to pursue these cases as victims and former officials have ended up in their countries.

signals.headline: Millions join funeral procession for Iran’s supreme leader Ali Khamenei
signals.summary: A crowd of “millions” assembled on Monday for the funeral procession of Iran’s assassinated supreme leader, Ali Khamenei .
keyTakeaways: Many will say the assembly was a monument to a misconceived war launched on Iran by Donald Trump in February.
The mourners wore black clothing and carried flags bearing the slogan “We will rise”; others held aloft the flag of Iran and pictures of Khamenei.
The Tehran metro was packed as people attempted to join the march.
signals.whyItMatters: Iranian officials said Khamenei’s absence was not due to wounds sustained in Israel’s attack on the presidential building but to concerns for his safety.

ja.headline: ハメネイ師の葬列に数百万人が参列
ja.summary: 暗殺されたイランの最高指導者アリー・ハメネイ師の葬儀行列が月曜日に執り行われ、数百万人ともいわれる群衆が首都テヘランに集まった。
keyTakeaways: この集会を、ドナルド・トランプが2月に仕掛けたとされる戦争への、民衆による無言の抗議と見る向きも多い。
参列者たちは黒い衣服をまとい、「我々は立ち上がる」と記された旗やイランの国旗、ハメネイ師の肖像を掲げた。
テヘランの地下鉄は行列に加わろうとする人々で混雑した。
ja.whyItMatters: イラン当局は、ハメネイ師の不在はイスラエルによる大統領府攻撃での負傷ではなく、身の安全への懸念によるものだと説明した。

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I'm seeing reports about a huge funeral procession in Iran. What happened?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Iran's supreme leader, Ali Khamenei, was assassinated. On Monday, a massive crowd—officials say millions—gathered in Tehran for his funeral procession.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Millions? That's enormous. What was the scene like?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Mourners wore black and carried flags with the slogan 'We will rise.' Many also held Iranian flags and pictures of Khamenei. The Tehran metro was packed with people trying to join the march.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: How is this being interpreted politically?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Some observers are calling the turnout a response to what they see as a misguided war launched on Iran by Donald Trump back in February.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Do we know more about how Khamenei died?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Iranian officials say his absence before this wasn't due to wounds from an Israeli attack on the presidential building, but rather concerns for his safety. The details around his assassination are still emerging.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: This feels like a major turning point for the region.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It is. Iran is now without its supreme leader, and the scale of this procession shows just how significant and uncertain this moment is.

signals.headline: FIFA World Cup: Mohamed Salah and the ghosts of Egypt’s ‘golden generation’
signals.summary: For some Egyptian football fans, watching Mohamed Salah qualify with Egypt for two World Cups in 2018 and 2026 will never make up for the fact that the “golden generation” did not get their shot in 2006, 2010 or 2014.
keyTakeaways: Salah is the captain and leader of the current generation.
Given his status, it is perhaps no surprise that Salah shouldered the blame for an AFCON drought that stretches back to 2010.
His achievement in dragging Egypt to the 2018 World Cup in Russia is brushed aside due to the fact that Egypt was the first team to be eliminated.
signals.whyItMatters: Salah might have been one of the best players in the Premier League, but Egypt fans were frustrated.

ja.headline: サラーと「黄金世代」の影——エジプトが抱えるW杯の記憶
ja.summary: モハメド・サラーが2018年と2026年、二度のワールドカップ出場をエジプトにもたらした。それでも一部のファンにとって、2006年から2014年にかけての「黄金世代」が本大会の舞台を踏めなかった事実は、いまも消えない痛みとして残っている。
keyTakeaways: 現世代の象徴として、サラーはキャプテンの重責を担い続けてきた。
2010年を最後に途絶えるアフリカネイションズカップの優勝から、その責任の一端はサラーに向けられてきた。
2018年ロシア大会への出場を牽引した功績も、グループステージ最下位での敗退という結果に覆われ、正当に評価されてこなかった。
ja.whyItMatters: プレミアリーグ屈指の選手であっても、エジプトのファンが抱く失望は容易には晴れなかった。

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: I saw something about Mohamed Salah and Egypt's football history. What's going on there?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It's an interesting tension. Salah led Egypt to two World Cups — 2018 and 2026 — which is a big deal. But for some Egyptian fans, that doesn't quite make up for something from the past.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What are they still upset about?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: There was a generation of players, called the 'golden generation,' who never made it to the World Cups in 2006, 2010, or 2014. For those fans, that's a lasting disappointment.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So even though Salah got them there, it's not enough?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right. And it's complicated by the fact that Egypt was eliminated first in 2018. Plus, the national team hasn't won the Africa Cup of Nations since 2010, and Salah's shouldered a lot of blame for that drought.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That seems harsh, given he's one of the best players in the Premier League.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It does. But that's the weight of expectation. He's the captain and leader now, so frustrations tend to land on him, fairly or not.

signals.headline: Inside the big business of the creator economy, with the agents making it happen
signals.summary: Half the people you’ve ever heard speak or perform or who show up anywhere have UTA agents representing them.
keyTakeaways: Here, UTA is helping creators literally launch products — and it’s fascinating that the stars of today are going from making media to making products of their own.
Of course, we also talked about AI, and platforms, both of which seem like destabilizing forces for the entire creator ecosystem.

I started my career at another agency, then after a few years, moved over to UTA in a very Jerry McGuire meets Entourage -esque moment and worked on the more traditional literary side of the business.
signals.whyItMatters: Which means I paid them money, making this a reverse conflict of interest.

ja.headline: クリエイター経済を動かすエージェントたちの仕事
ja.summary: あなたがこれまでに耳にしたことのある語り手や表現者の多くは、UTAというエージェンシーに代理されている。クリエイター経済の裏側で、何が起きているのか。
keyTakeaways: UTAはいまやクリエイターの商品開発まで手がける——メディアを作る側が、自らのブランドや製品を持つ時代への移行は興味深い
AIとプラットフォームの変化は、クリエイター全体のエコシステムを揺るがす力として議論に上った
語り手自身は別のエージェンシーでキャリアを始め、数年後にUTAへ移籍。出版畑の仕事を担ってきた
ja.whyItMatters: 語り手はUTAに報酬を支払う立場にあり、いわば逆の利益相反が生じている。

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So there's this whole world of agents representing online creators now?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes. UTA, one of the big talent agencies, is deeply involved in the creator economy — representing people who built their careers online.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: What does that actually look like? What are these agents doing?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: They're helping creators launch their own products. So it's not just about making videos or podcasts anymore — these stars are creating physical goods and brands.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's a big shift from just making content.

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. And the conversation also touched on AI and social media platforms — both seen as potentially destabilizing forces for creators right now.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Destabilizing how?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: The piece doesn't spell it out, but those technologies are changing how content gets made and distributed, which affects how creators build careers and earn money.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And this story came from someone who actually worked at UTA?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right. They started at another agency, then moved to UTA and worked on the traditional literary side before getting into this world.

signals.headline: Nintendo will stop selling the original Switch in Europe next year
signals.summary: The decision comes alongside an update to the Switch 2 to coincide with European battery regulations.
keyTakeaways: Nintendo is making a new version of the Switch 2 with a replaceable battery in Europe — but its predecessor has a very different future.
As part of an updated FAQ about revisions to Nintendo hardware in Europe, the company confirmed that it will stop selling all iterations of the original Switch on the continent starting next year.

From mid-February 2027, almost ten years after Nintendo Switch launched in March 2017, Nintendo will no longer sell to retailers hardware in the Nintendo Switch family of systems — specifically Nintendo Switch, Nintendo Switch Lite, and Nintendo Switch – OLED Model.
signals.whyItMatters: The Switch 2 is the most notable product being updated — the new version is expected to start rolling out in the fall — but there will also be versions of the Joy-Con controllers, Joy-Con 2, Switch 2 Pro Controller, and N64 and GameCube Switch controllers with user-replaceable batteries.

ja.headline: 任天堂、初代Switchの欧州販売を来年終了へ
ja.summary: 欧州のバッテリー規制への対応として、任天堂はSwitch 2の新仕様を発表した。それと同時に、初代Switchシリーズの販売終了も明らかになった。
keyTakeaways: 欧州向けSwitch 2は交換可能なバッテリーを搭載した新仕様に移行する。一方、初代Switchシリーズの行方は対照的だ。
任天堂は欧州向けハードウェアに関するFAQを更新し、初代Switchの全モデルについて来年から欧州での販売を終了すると正式に認めた。
2027年2月中旬以降、Nintendo Switch・Switch Lite・Switch（有機ELモデル）のいずれも、欧州の小売店への出荷が停止される。Switch発売から約10年の節目となる。
ja.whyItMatters: 今回の規制対応はSwitch 2本体にとどまらず、Joy-Con 2やSwitch 2 Proコントローラー、N64・ゲームキューブ対応コントローラーなど周辺機器にも及ぶ見通しで、秋ごろからの順次展開が予想されている。

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: So Nintendo's stopping sales of the original Switch in Europe?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Yes, starting mid-February next year. That includes the regular Switch, the Lite, and the OLED model.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: That's almost ten years since it first launched, right?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Exactly. The Switch came out in March 2017, so it'll be just shy of a decade.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Why now though? Is this just about the Switch 2?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Partly. There's a bigger reason — new European battery regulations. Nintendo's making a version of the Switch 2 with a replaceable battery to comply.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: Oh, so users can swap the battery themselves?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: Right. That updated Switch 2 is expected to roll out in the fall. They're also updating Joy-Con controllers and some other accessories the same way.

captions.speaker: listener
captions.text: And the original Switch just doesn't meet those rules?

captions.speaker: explainer
captions.text: It seems Nintendo decided it's simpler to phase it out rather than redesign older hardware. The focus is clearly on the new generation now.
